title: Callaloo
subtitle: Caribbean leafy-green stew with okra and coconut milk
excerpt: A silky, spiced stew of leafy greens and okra cooked down in coconut milk with scotch bonnet, thyme, and spring onion. Eaten as a side across Jamaica and Trinidad, with variations in every island.
sourceNotes: Callaloo is a dish common across the Caribbean, though the term refers to different plants depending on the island: in Jamaica it means the leaves of the amaranth plant; in Trinidad it means taro (dasheen) leaves. Spinach is the most widely available substitute in the UK and gives a broadly similar result, though true callaloo leaves have a earthier, more robust flavour. Note: a dedicated callaloo ingredient should be added to the master ingredient table. This recipe uses spinach as a substitute.

content.text: Callaloo is not one dish but several, each island's cooks working with different leaves and in different proportions. In Jamaica, the leaves go in with onion, scotch bonnet, and thyme, and the result is a dark, softly wilted side that accompanies ackee and saltfish at breakfast or jerk chicken at dinner. The Trinidadian version is creamier, made with dasheen leaves and more coconut milk. Both versions share the same aromatic base.

Spinach gives a broadly faithful result when callaloo is unavailable. The okra thickens the stew slightly; leave it out if you dislike the texture, but it is traditional. The whole dish comes together in under 30 minutes and is as simple as any greens side dish.

items.name: Spinach
items.prepNote: callaloo leaves if available, otherwise fresh spinach

items.name: Scotch bonnet
items.prepNote: whole, or finely chopped for more heat

content.text: Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme sprigs, and the whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Cook for 2 minutes.

Add the {{okra}} of sliced okra, stir to combine with the aromatics, and cook for 3 minutes. Add the {{spinach}} of spinach leaves in batches, stirring each addition into the pot until wilted before adding the next. Pour over the {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and stir well. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt.

Cover and simmer over medium-low heat for 10 minutes, stirring once halfway through. Remove the scotch bonnet and thyme before serving. The callaloo should be a dark, slightly creamy stew, not a dry sauté.

items.fix: Remove the lid and increase the heat to medium for 5 minutes to evaporate the excess liquid
items.cause: Spinach released a lot of liquid; heat too low

items.fix: Slice okra into thin rounds and cook at a higher temperature initially before adding the coconut milk. The sliminess reduces with heat and becomes a thickener rather than a texture problem
items.cause: Sliced too thick; cooked in acidic liquid without prior browning

items.fix: Add a teaspoon of dark soy sauce or Worcestershire sauce to deepen the flavour. True callaloo leaves have an earthy, slightly bitter quality that spinach lacks
items.cause: Spinach lacks the depth of true callaloo

items.fix: Stir in an extra tablespoon of coconut milk or a spoonful of plain yoghurt to cool it down

content.text: Callaloo is one of the most widely eaten dishes across the Caribbean. It shows up in some form in nearly every island's cooking. In Jamaica it most often appears at breakfast next to ackee and saltfish, as the green side of the meal. In Trinidad, callaloo with coconut milk and crab is a big part of Sunday cooking, served with pelau and macaroni pie. The UK version using spinach is close, but it misses the earthy note that true dasheen or amaranth leaves bring.
